_notice: Corpus data `kind` -> the SHIPPED scanner detector name(s) that mean the SAME datum. A match whose detector name is NOT in this list is a WRONG-KIND match: it does not count as identification or as coverage for any framework, because redacting a thing you mis-identified is luck, not detection, and the compliance claim it would earn is false.
_source: Detector names are the `name` field of every entry in `ALL_PATTERNS`, exported by the SHIPPED engine `@pretense/scanner` (pretense/packages/scanner). The bridge loads that list from the BUILT dist at run time and VALIDATES this file against it.
_enforcement: run.mjs::validateKindDetectors hard-fails (exit 2, no numbers printed) if ANY of: (1) a detector named here is not exported by the shipped engine; (2) a shipped detector is claimed by no kind and is not triaged in `unmapped_detectors`; (3) the engine ships a detector whose NAME means a kind (`<kind>` or `<kind>-*`, with `_`->`-`) that the kind does not list and `rejected_name_matches` does not explain; (4) a kind maps to [] without an entry in `kinds_without_shipped_detector`. This file cannot go quietly stale: rule (3) is exactly what pretense #521 tripped when it shipped `npi-labeled` / `uk-nhs-number-labeled` / `email-labeled` / `github-token-split` while this map still said those kinds had no detector, costing the product ~4.0 points it had actually earned.
_judgment_calls: Where a kind is deliberately NOT credited for a detector that fires on its cases, the reason is written down: `rejected_name_matches` for name-similar detectors, `wrong_kind_notes` for everything else. An empty list or a missing entry is never shorthand for 'we decided this' — it is a hard failure.

kinds_without_shipped_detector.advertising_id: The shipped engine has NO detector for mobile advertising IDs (IDFA/GAID — bare UUIDs). Every such case is a guaranteed MISS, and that is the honest result. A bare-UUID detector would be a large false-positive source, so this is a defensible product decision rather than an oversight — but it IS an uncovered kind for the GDPR/CCPA/LGPD-class 'online identifier' obligations this corpus maps it to.
kinds_without_shipped_detector.imei: The shipped engine has NO IMEI detector. IMEIs are 15-digit Luhn-valid numbers, so a detector would overlap the card/phone space; the engine deliberately does not claim them. Uncovered for GDPR/CCPA device-identifier obligations.

wrong_kind_notes._what: Detectors OBSERVED firing on a kind's cases that this map deliberately does NOT credit to that kind. These are the residual 23 wrong-kind cases in the report (imei 10, advertising_id 10, health_record 1, jwt 1, iban 1 on engine 122aec3). Crediting any of them would manufacture compliance coverage for data the engine detected as a different element — the framework control the credit implies is not actually satisfied. Each entry names the detector that really fires, so the judgment can be re-argued from evidence rather than re-guessed.
wrong_kind_notes.imei: Fires: `credit-card` (10 cases). NOT credited. An IMEI is a 15-digit Luhn-valid number, so the card detector claims it. The value does get redacted in practice, but as a payment card — no device-identifier control under GDPR/CCPA is satisfied by a card finding, and the audit trail would state the wrong data class.
wrong_kind_notes.advertising_id: Fires: `credit-card` (10 of the wrong-kind cases; 16 matches across tiers). NOT credited, same reasoning as `imei`: an advertising ID recognized as a payment card earns no online-identifier coverage.
wrong_kind_notes.health_record: Fires: `phone-us` and `email` on one case — the contact lines of a clinical record, not the diagnosis/MRN the case tests for. NOT credited: HIPAA coverage for the record is not earned by catching the patient's phone number while the diagnosis egresses in plaintext.
wrong_kind_notes.jwt: Fires: `generic-secret-assignment` on one case. NOT credited: the assignment match and a `jwt-token` match have different spans, so counting it conflates 'we caught the variable' with 'we caught the token'.
wrong_kind_notes.iban: Fires: `credit-card` on one case (an IBAN whose digit run is Luhn-valid). NOT credited: it is redacted as a payment card, which is the wrong data class for the financial-data control the case maps to.

unmapped_detectors._what: SHIPPED detectors that no corpus kind claims. Each needs a written reason so a NEWLY-shipped detector cannot land here silently — the bridge fails until someone triages it. Being listed here has NO effect on any score; these detectors simply have no corresponding corpus cases.
unmapped_detectors.circleci-token: CI/CD vendor token. The corpus has no CircleCI case; the generic `secret` / `api_key` kinds cover the class.
unmapped_detectors.datadog-api-key: Observability vendor token. No corpus case.
unmapped_detectors.digitalocean-token: Cloud vendor token. No corpus case.
unmapped_detectors.docker-registry: Registry auth blob. No corpus case.
unmapped_detectors.gitlab-token: SCM vendor token. The corpus exercises GitHub only (`github_token`, `github_finegrained_pat`).
unmapped_detectors.heroku-api-key: PaaS vendor token. No corpus case.
unmapped_detectors.launchdarkly-mobile-key: Feature-flag vendor token. No corpus case.
unmapped_detectors.launchdarkly-sdk-key: Feature-flag vendor token. No corpus case.
unmapped_detectors.mailgun-key: Email vendor token. The corpus exercises SendGrid only (`sendgrid_key`).
unmapped_detectors.newrelic-key: Observability vendor token. No corpus case.
unmapped_detectors.pagerduty-key: Paging vendor token. No corpus case.
unmapped_detectors.stripe-webhook-secret: The corpus `api_key` / `stripe_restricted_key` kinds exercise Stripe's key formats, not the webhook signing secret.
unmapped_detectors.travis-token: CI vendor token. No corpus case.
unmapped_detectors.x509-certificate: Certificate body. The corpus exercises private keys (`pgp_private_key`, `ssh_private_key`), not certificates — a certificate is public material.
